coredata.dc:title: Nanosilver-decorated covalent organic frameworks for enhanced photodynamic, photothermal, and antibacterial properties
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yong
Wan, Yating
Zeng, Zijian
Yan, Zhihong
Liu, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Bacterial infections caused by multidrug-resistant bacteria pose an extremely serious treat to human health However, nanoscale antibacterial materials can effectively inhibit bacterial growth without inducing bacterial resistance. Herein, we have developed a highly effective antibacterial agent by immobilizing silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) within a novel covalent organic framework (COF). The COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag system was synthesized in situ using BODIPY phoyosensitizer (BDP) and carbon quantum dots (CD-S-3) as precursors. The optimized COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag system exhibited excellent photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT). Importantly, the COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag system, with many active groups, can capture and kill bacteria effectively by interacting with them, thereby contributing to improved antibacterial efficacy. As expected, the designed COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag system combines the advantages of COFBDP/CD-S-3 (preventing the aggregation of AgNPs and having the ability to capture bacteria) and AgNPs (antibacterial ability), demonstrating excellent antibacterial efficacy. This study developed a new class of antibacterial materials and took an important step forward in promoting the application of COF-based antibacterial materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ag nanoparticles
Nanocomposites
Photodynamic effect
Photothermal effect
Antibacterial

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) SEM images of COFBDP/CD-S-3 with different magnified folds; (c, d) SEM and (e, f) TEM images of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag with different magnified folds; (g, h, i) EDS elemental mapping picture of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag.

(a) FT-IR patterns of COFBDP/CD-S-3 and COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag; (b and c) Survey spectra of COFBDP/CD-S-3 and COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag; (d) N 1s XPS spectrum of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag; (e) S 2p XPS spectrum of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag; (f) Ag 3d XPS spectrum of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag.

(a) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of COFBDP/CD-S-3 at 77 K; (b) the pore size distribution of COFBDP/CD-S-3; (c) BET ratio surface calculation diagram of COFBDP/CD-S-3; (d) TGA curves of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag and COFBDP/CD-S-3.

Under the irradiation of 420 nm (0.6 W/cm2) laser, (a) the absorbance change of DPBF (25 μ mol·L−1) at 410 nm after adding COFBDP/CD-S-3 and COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag (50 μg mL−1) and (b) that of DPBF (25 μmol L−1) at 410 nm after adding different concentrations of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag; (c) Under the irradiation of 808 nm (3 W/cm2) laser, the absorbance change of DPBF (25 μmol L−1) at 410 nm after adding COFBDP/CD-S-3 and COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag (50 μg mL−1).

Under the irradiation of 808 nm (3 W/cm2) laser, (a) the temperature change of COFBDP/CD-S-3 (0.25 mg mL−1) and COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag (0.25 mg mL−1, 0.035 mg mL−1); (b) The heating/cooling cycles of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag (0.25 mg mL−1) over 4 cycles of laser illumination; (c) The temperature variation by primary heating and cooling of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag (0.25 mg mL−1); (d) The plot of linear time data versus −ln(θ) from the cooling stage; (e) Under the irradiation of 420 nm (3W) laser, the temperature change of COFBDP/CD-S-3 and COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag (0.25 mg mL−1).
Hemolysis experiment of different concentrations of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag and COFBDP/CD-S-3.
The schematic representation for preparing COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag.
The MIC values of COFBDP/CD-S-3 and COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag to four common bacteria under the condition of 420 nm (3 W/cm2) laser irradiation for different time.
The MIC values of COFBDP/CD-S-3 and COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag to four common bacteria under the condition of 808 nm (3 W/cm2) laser irradiation for different time.
The MIC values of COFBDP/CD-S-3 and COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag to four kinds of common bacteria after 808 nm (3 W/cm2) laser irradiation on 30 min and 420 nm (3 W/cm2) laser irradiation on 8 min.
Hemolysis rate of different concentrations of COFBDP/CD-S-3 and COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag.

head.ce:title: Nanosilver-decorated covalent organic frameworks for enhanced photodynamic, photothermal, and antibacterial properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medicine and Chemical Engineer, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Zhongshan, Guangdong, 528458, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Medicine and Chemical Engineer

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Medicine and Chemical Engineer, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Zhongshan, Guangdong, 528458, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Guangzhou, Guangdong, 510006, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of pharmacy, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Guangzhou, Guangdong, 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Advanced Drug Delivery Systems and Guangdong Provincial Engineering Center of Topical Precise Drug Delivery System, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Guangzhou, Guangdong, 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Advanced Drug Delivery Systems and Guangdong Provincial Engineering Center of Topical Precise Drug Delivery System

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Advanced Drug Delivery Systems and Guangdong Provincial Engineering Center of Topical Precise Drug Delivery System, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Guangzhou, Guangdong, 510006, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Zhongshan, 528458, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial infections caused by multidrug-resistant bacteria pose an extremely serious treat to human health However, nanoscale antibacterial materials can effectively inhibit bacterial growth without inducing bacterial resistance. Herein, we have developed a highly effective antibacterial agent by immobilizing silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) within a novel covalent organic framework (COF). The COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag system was synthesized in situ using BODIPY phoyosensitizer (BDP) and carbon quantum dots (CD-S-3) as precursors. The optimized COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag system exhibited excellent photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT). Importantly, the COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag system, with many active groups, can capture and kill bacteria effectively by interacting with them, thereby contributing to improved antibacterial efficacy. As expected, the designed COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag system combines the advantages of COFBDP/CD-S-3 (preventing the aggregation of AgNPs and having the ability to capture bacteria) and AgNPs (antibacterial ability), demonstrating excellent antibacterial efficacy. This study developed a new class of antibacterial materials and took an important step forward in promoting the application of COF-based antibacterial materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: For a long time, bacterial and fungal infections have posed a serious threat to human life and health [1]. With the prevalence of antibiotic resistance and the lack of improved antibiotics, modern antibacterial methods have been increasingly studied [2]. Metal nanoparticles, such as silver, gold, plutonium, and palladium, are attractive candidates for antibacterial nanomaterials, mainly due to their excellent optical properties [3–7]. In addition to being able to disrupt the bacterial cell membrane [8] and inhibit bacterial enzymes [9], AgNPs can also release Ag+ as a catalytic center to induce the production of highly reactive oxygen species and exhibit photodynamic antibacterial effects [10]. Furthermore, AgNPs possess significant local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) characteristics [11], which can convert photon energy into thermal energy under near-infrared light ( = 650∼1100 nm), leading to the generation of local extreme temperatures that can cause bacterial rupture and death [12].

The cytotoxicity and aggregation of AgNPs are not conducive to its application in the field of antibacterial, therefore, it is necessary to choose a suitable carrier to form nanocomposite to overcome these shortcomings. Covalent organic frameworks (COFs) are porous conjugated polymers formed by strong covalent bonds connecting light elements (such as hydrogen, carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, silicon, etc.) [13,14], typically exhibiting a certain degree of crystallinity and larger specific surface area [15–17]. Compared to metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), COFs possess higher thermal and chemical stability without collapsing in the solvent [18]. Moreover, the structural and chemical control of COFs can be achieved through the pre-design of the organic monomers that form them [19], making them an ideal carrier for loading various optically active molecules and nanomaterials [20].

It has been reported that COFs are mostly constructed by organic molecules with multiple functional groups, and the COFs using carbon dots as monomers are rare [21,22]. Carbon dots are novel carbon-based nanomaterials with ultra-small size and fluorescent features. Due to their photochemical stability, facile surface functionalization, and minimal toxicity, carbon dots hold immense potential for a wide range of applications, including sensing, biomedicine, photocatalysis, among others [23–28].

In this paper, we synthesized a novel type of nanocomposite, COFBDP/CD-S-3, by reacting a BODIPY molecule (BDP) with S-element-doped carbon dots (CD-S-3) modified with an aldehyde group using a Schiff base reaction. Subsequently, we obtained COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag by incorporating AgNPs using an in-situ reduction method (Scheme 1 ). COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag offers several advantages: Firstly, COFBDP/CD-S-3 acts as a carrier that ensures good biosafety of the composites. Secondly, the S element and porous structure of COFBDP/CD-S-3 prevent AgNPs from aggregating and improve their stability. Finally, the PDT and PTT effects of AgNPs at specific wavelengths effectively kill Gram-positive bacteria, Gram-negative bacteria, and fungi. Therefore, COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag holds immense potential as a broad-spectrum antibacterial material, which could potentially overcome the challenges of antibiotic abuse and drug resistance.

P-dimethylaminobenzaldehyde (≥99.7%), 2,4-dimethyl pyrrole (≥97%) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (≥97%), Glutaraldehyde (50% in H2O), Thiophene (≥99%) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tetrakis (triphenylphosphine) palladium (≥99%) was purchased from Beijing oka Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing China). Silver nitrate (≥99.7%) was obtained from Tianjin Yuchuan Reagent No.3 Factory (Tianjin, China). Glycerol (≥99.5%) was purchased from Shandong Yousuo Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China). Escherichia coil (E. coli), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), and Monilia Albican were bought from Guangdong microbial strain Preservation Center (Guangdong, China). Lennox and Sabouraud Dextrose Broth were purchased from Guangdong Huankai microbial Technology Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Defibred sheep blood was bought from Henan Yuechi Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Henan, China). All reagents were used directly without further purification. The water used in all the experiments was produced by an RO-400G Microcomputer water quality processor (Guangdong hemisphere industrial group Co., Ltd).

Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) of the samples were gained on an IRAffinity-1 infrared microspectrometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd.). The material morphology was collected using a JSM-7610F PLUS Scanning electron microscope (JEOL Co., Ltd.) and an H-7650 Transmission electron microscope (Japan Hitachi, LTD). XPS spectrum was obtained on an AXIS NOVA X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd.). The BET experiment was accomplished using V-Sorb 2800p specific surface area and aperture analyzer (Beijing Jinepu Technology Co., Ltd.). TGA curves were observed by using an SDT650 synchronous thermal analyzer (TA Instrument). 420 nm light source was purchased from Jilin New Industry Laser Co., Ltd. 808 nm light source was bought from Youjing Optoelectronic original Factory. The absorbance was determined by UV-2600 ultraviolet spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd.). The real-time temperature of the material was measured by a DS-2TPH10-3AUF thermal camera (Hangzhou Haikang Weishi Digital Technology Co., Ltd.).

Glutaraldehyde (300 μL), thiophene (900 μL), and glycerol (30 μL) were added to a Teflon reaction kettle and heated in an oven at 150 °C for 3 h. After being cooled down, acetone and chloroform were added to the resultant brown liquid and centrifuged at 10 000 rpm for 15 min to remove high-weight carbon aggregates. This procedure was repeated three times for each solvent and finally evaporated under reduced pressure to obtain a brown solid.

BDP (8 mg), CD-S-3 (20 mg), and acetic acid (50 μL) were mixed in 16 mL ethanol and stirred for 24 h. The solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to obtain the purplish red products, washed three times with ethanol, and finally dried under vacuum to obtain COFBDP / CD-S-3.

COFBDP/CD-S-3 (10 mg) was dispersed in 10 mL methanol. After sonicated for 0.5 h, AgNO3 aqueous solution (200 mmol L−1, 5 mL) was added to the dispersion, sonicated without light for 1 h, and the purplish-red solid was obtained after centrifuging at 10 000 rpm for 10 min. After washing with methanol three times, the purplish red solid was redispersed in 10 mL methanol, then added NaBH4 methanol solution (200 mmol L−1, 5 mL), stirred for 48 h, centrifuged and washed with methanol three times, and finally dried under vacuum to obtain COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag.

The bacteria were grown overnight in liquid medium at 37 °C and then diluted with liquid medium to approximately 2.0 × 105 CFU/mL. The bacterial suspensions was added to each hole of the 96 well cell culture plates and mixed with different concentrations of sample dispersion in equal volume, incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. Each assay was carried out in triplicate. The inhibited visible growth of bacterial cells was assessed by turbidimertric method.

After overnight incubation at 37 °C, the bacterial suspensions were diluted with liquid medium and grew to approximate 5.0 × 106 CFU/mL. The bacterial suspension was mixed with different concentrations of sample dispersion, and 65 μL of the mixed liquid was swabbed onto the surface of solid medium and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. Each assay was carried out in triplicate.

The 2% red blood cell suspension was added to the centrifuge tube and mixed with different concentrations of sample dispersion in equal volume, incubate at 37 °C for 1 h in the incubator, observe the hemolysis of each group. The Supernatant fluid was obtained after centrifuging at 5000 rpm for 10 min, measured its absorbance at 540 nm, and calculated the hemolysis rate according to the formula: Hemolysis rate (%) = [(ODsample - ODnegative)/(ODpositive - ODnegative)] × 100%

Fig. 1 a and b, and c, d are SEM images of COFBDP/CD-S-3 and COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag, respectively. It can be seen that the shape of COFBDP/CD-S-3 was mainly uniform spherical, with a particle size of about 700–800 nm. Compared with COFBDP/CD-S-3, COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag had a rougher surface and the presence of particles. The SEM image of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag (Fig. 1e) showed that the AgNPs grown in situ are uniformly distributed in the surface and pores of COFBDP/CD-S-3. In addition, Fig. 1f is the high-resolution electron microscopic image of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag, showing the atomic lattice stripes belonging to the Ag (200) plane with a spacing of 0.205 nm. The elemental mapping of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag (Fig. 1g and h, i) showed that S and Ag elements existed and distributed uniformly in the composites, which proves the successful preparation of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag.

The FT-IR spectra of BDP, CD-S-3, and COFBDP/CD-S-3 was shown in Fig. 2 a. The characteristic peak of the BDP at 3385 cm−1 was caused by the stretching vibration of N – H, and the stretching vibration of C = O in the CD-S-3 structure leads to the appearance of a characteristic peak at 1721 cm−1. Compared with BDP and CD-S-3, the spectrum of COFBDP/CD-S-3 showed a different characteristic peak at 1655 cm−1 (C = N), while the characteristic peak belonging to N – H and C = O weakened or even disappeared, which proves that the condensation reaction took place and COFBDP/CD-S-3 was synthesized successfully [22].

The types and existing states of elements in the composites were analyzed by XPS. Compared with COFBDP/CD-S-3 (Fig. 2b), COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag (Fig. 2c) had the peak of Ag element. Fig. 2f showed the map of Ag 3d, with two peaks of 368.2 eV (Ag 3d5/2) and 374.2 eV (Ag 3d3/2), which proves that the Ag element was successfully loaded into COFBDP/CD-S-3 in the form of Ag0. Fig. 2d shows the N 1s of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag with a peak at 399.80 eV belonging to C=N, which further validates the occurrence of the condensation reaction. The XPS spectrum of S 2p in COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag can be fitted to three peaks, including S 2p3/2 at 161.52 eV, S 2p1/2 at 162.55 eV and C–S–C at 164.24 eV. The characteristic peaks of S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 coincide with the metal sulfur bond (Ag–S), indicating that the S element in COFBDP/CD-S-3 can combine the ith Ag element, which is helpful to the in situ loading of AgNPs [29–33].

The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of COFBDP/CD-S-3 at 77 K followed the type Ⅱ nitrogen adsorption isotherm (Fig. 3 a), and the small hysteresis loop in the figure was caused by the formation of intercrystalline gaps in the microspheres [34]. As shown by Fig. 3b, the pore size of COFBDP/CD-S-3 was about 4.2 nm, which belonged to mesoporous material. The R2 in the BET result diagrams of COFBDP/CD-S-3 was 0.999, and the specific surface area of COFBDP/CD-S-3 is 14.25 m2 g−1 (Fig. 3c). TAG experiments were carried out on COFBDP/CD-S-3 and COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag (Fig. 3d), it was observed that their structures did not collapse when the temperature was lower than 207.14 °C, which indicated that both of their good thermal stability.

The photodynamic properties of COFBDP/CD-S-3 and COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag were measured by using DPBF as a 1O2 probe. Under the irradiation of 420 nm laser, compared with the control group, the absorbance of DPBF in COFBDP/CD-S-3 and COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag groups decreased significantly, indicating that they produced 1O2 and had certain photodynamic properties, and COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag has stronger performance and photodynamic antibacterial potential (Fig. 4 a). Further determination of different concentrations of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag dispersion showed that the ability of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag to produce 1O2 was partly affected by the concentration. The reason for this phenomenon is that when the concentration of the dispersion was higher, the dispersed COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag particles in the system blocked the irradiation of the 420 nm laser, which reduced the laser utilization of the system. The best dispersion concentration of 1O2 is 50 μg mL−1 (Fig. 4b). By contrast, the ability of COFBDP/CD-S-3 and COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag to produce 1O2 under 808 nm laser irradiation was studied, and the results showed that their photodynamic properties are not good (Fig. 4c).

The photothermal properties of COFBDP/CD-S-3 and COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag were evaluated by measuring the temperature change after irradiation with an 808 nm laser at different time points. Specifically, as shown in Fig. 5 a and Fig. S1, the temperature change of COFBDP/CD-S-3 (0.2 mg mL−1) was similar to that of the control, whereas COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag (0.25 mg mL−1) exhibited a significant increase in temperature (ΔT up to 22 °C) after 10 min, revealing that the photothermal performance of the hybrid system was significantly improved after the Ag deposition. Additionally, the photothermal property of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag was found to be concentration-dependent, as evidenced by a decrease in ΔT at a lower concentrat ion (0.035 mg mL−1) compared to that at 0.25 mg mL−1. The repeatable heating/cooling curve of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag showed no significant decrease in temperature during the four irradiation cycles, indicating good photothermal stability (Fig. 5b). The photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag was determined by taking the cooling period of a repeatable heating/cooling curve as a linear regression curve and calculating η using the Roper formula, which was found to be 8.95% (Fig. 5c and d) [35].

Furthermore, when the light source was changed to a 420 nm laser, no significant change in temperature was observed for COFBDP/CD-S-3 and COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag, indicating the absence of photothermal properties under this condition (Fig. 5e). These results suggest that COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag has potential as a photothermal agent, which could be further explored for its antibacterial and therapeutic applications.

To assess the antibacterial activity of COFBDP/CD-S-3 and COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag, the microdilution method was used to determine the minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) under various experimental conditions. When irradiated by a 420 nm laser, the MIC values of the two compounds were determined and presented in Table 1 . Notably, the antibacterial effect of COFBDP/CD-S-3 was found to be unaffected by the extension of laser irradiation time, whereas the antibacterial activity of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag against four common bacterial strains gradually increased. Although COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag did not exhibit photothermal properties under the 420 nm laser irradiation, it was observed to produce singlet oxygen (1O2) and had a photodynamic effect. The result shows that under the irradiation of 420 nm laser, COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag can achieve antibacterial effect by producing 1O2, which is consistent with the report that AgNPs releases Ag+ and then produces 1O2 to destroy bacterial cells. Therefore, the primary antibacterial mechanism of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag under the 420 nm laser irradiation was the result of photodynamic action [36].

Upon substituting the light source with an 808 nm laser, the antibacterial activity of each group was evaluated, and the results were documented in Table 2 . Notably, no significant antibacterial effect was observed for any group within the initial 10 min of illumination. However, upon extending the illumination time to 20 min and 30 min, COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag demonstrated a notable antibacterial effect with a lower MIC. Under the 808 nm laser irradiation, COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag was observed to have a remarkable photothermal effect, characterized by a sharp increase in temperature, while its ability to generate singlet oxygen was suboptimal. The results show that under the irradiation of 808 nm laser, COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag has photothermal effect, which can lead to the destruction of bacterial cell wall and cell membrane, and finally achieve antibacterial effect, therefore, the primary antibacterial activity of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag under these experimental conditions is attributed to its photothermal effect [37].

Based on the data presented in Table 3 , it is evident that the antibacterial activity of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag, when irradiated by 808 nm laser on 30 min followed by 8 min of 420 nm laser irradiation, is marginally superior to that observed with a single 30 min 808 nm laser treatment. These findings suggest that COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag exhibits a combined antibacterial effect that results from the addition of both photothermal and photodynamic mechanisms when exposed to two distinct light sources. It is noteworthy that the plate experiment results provide additional verification of the significant potential of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag for efficacious antibacterial therapy through the synergistic combination of PTT and PDT (Fig. S2).

In conclusion, we successfully synthesized a S-doped COF material (COFBDP/CD-S-3) through the combination of BDP and CD-S-3. The presence of sulfur in the porous structure of COFBDP/CD-S-3 facilitated the in-situ growth of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), resulting in the formation of COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag composites. Under 420 nm laser irradiation, COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag exhibited efficient generation of singlet oxygen (1O2), leading to a significant bactericidal effect via photodynamic therapy (PDT). Meanwhile, under 808 nm laser irradiation, the composites showed remarkable photothermal conversion efficiency (η = 8.95%) and a temperature increase (ΔT) of up to 22 °C, indicating the potential for photothermal therapy (PTT) in sterilization. Furthermore, the COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag composites demonstrated excellent stability over four repeated heating/cooling cycles and good biocompatibility. Due to the PDT and PTT of the COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag composites, as well as its inherent ability to capture/kill bacteria, an environmentally friendly, easy-to-use, broad-spectrum, and highly efficient antibacterial platform has been achieved. Therefore, our findings suggest that COFBDP/CD-S-3-Ag composites have promising applications in biomedicine.

Yong Liu: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Yating Wan: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Zijian Zeng: Data curation. Zhihong Yan: Writing – review & editing. Yi Liu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Guangdong Provincial Natural Science of Foundation of China (No.2019A1515011687), and the Innovation Training Program of China (No.202210573003). It was also supported by the Science & Technology Plan Projects of Yantai, China (No.2019R1015-1)

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2023.128158.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The interrelationships between antimicrobial resistance, COVID-19, past, and future pandemics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ukuhor H O. The interrelationships between antimicrobial resistance, COVID-19, past, and future pandemics. J. infect. Public Heal., 14 (2021), 53-60.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibiotic resistance in microbes: history, mechanisms, therapeutic strategies and future prospects

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Uddin T M, Chakraborty A J, Khusro A, et al. Antibiotic resistance in microbes: History, mechanisms, therapeutic strategies and future prospects. J. Infect. Public Heal., 14 (2021), 1750-1766.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanomaterials and nanotechnology for water treatment: recent advances

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Iravani S. Nanomaterials and nanotechnology for water treatment: recent advances. Inorg. Nano-Met. Chem., 51 (2021), 1615-1645.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of Cu-nanoparticle hydrogel with self-healing and photothermal properties

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen S, Tang F, Tang L Z, et al. Synthesis of Cu-Nanoparticle Hydrogel with Self-Healing and Photothermal Properties. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces, 9 (2017), 20895-20903.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effective PDT/PTT dual-modal phototherapeutic killing of pathogenic bacteria by using ruthenium nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Huang X Q, Chen G J, Pan J L, et al. Effective PDT/PTT dual-modal phototherapeutic killing of pathogenic bacteria by using ruthenium nanoparticles. J. Mater. Chem. B, 4 (2016), 6258-6270.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Two-dimensional antibacterial Pd@Ag nanosheets with a synergetic effect of plasmonic heating and Ag+ release

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Mo S G, Chen X L, Chen M, et al. Two-dimensional antibacterial Pd@Ag nanosheets with a synergetic effect of plasmonic heating and Ag+ release. J. Mater. Chem. B, 3 (2015), 6255-6260.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Embelin-mediated green synthesis of quasi-spherical and StarShaped plasmonic nanostructures for antibacterial activity, photothermal therapy, and computed tomographic imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sasidharan S, Poojari R, Bahadur D, et al. Embelin-Mediated Green Synthesis of Quasi-Spherical and StarShaped Plasmonic Nanostructures for Antibacterial Activity, Photothermal Therapy, and Computed Tomographic Imaging. ACS Sustain. Chem. Eng., 6 (2018), 10562-10577.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multivalent and synergistic chitosan oligosaccharide-Ag nanocomposites for therapy of bacterial infection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li M, Xu Z L, Shi Y m, et al. Multivalent and synergistic chitosan oligosaccharide-Ag nanocomposites for therapy of bacterial infection. Sci. Rep. 10 (2020), 10011.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The bactericidal effect of silver nanoparticles
